answers.value: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and displacement risk; construction vibration; shade and shadow effects on adjacent neighborhoods; emergency access and evacuation constraints due to proximity to the American River and levee system.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Present Land Use Zoning General Plan Designation
answers.value:
Present land use: Older light industrial warehouses, vehicle storage yards, and a small cluster of single-story commercial buildings with associated surface parking; several parcels are currently vacant or used for informal outdoor storage.

Zoning: Predominantly M-1 (Light Industrial) and C-2 (General Commercial) with a small portion designated R-2 (Medium Density Residential) along 7th Street.

General Plan designation: Employment Mixed Use (EMU) and General Commercial (GC) with a narrow band of Urban Residential (UR) along the northern boundary adjacent to existing single-family neighborhoods.

Surrounding uses: To the north, existing single-family residential neighborhoods and Northside Elementary School; to the south, the American River and Riverview Canal with a maintenance road along the levee; to the east, a small business park and Riverview Municipal Airport flight path; to the west, older commercial strip development along E. Riverfront Drive.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The North Riverfront Mixed-Use Redevelopment Project proposes to transform approximately 18.4 acres of underutilized and partially vacant industrial and commercial land along the American River in the City of Riverview into a compact, walkable, mixed-use riverfront district that combines new housing, neighborhood-serving retail, office employment, and public open space.

The project would construct up to 285 new residential units in a mix of formats, including three 5-story podium-style apartment buildings (approximately 210 units) oriented toward the internal main street and riverfront, and 75 3-story townhome and stacked-flat units along the northern project edge to provide a compatible transition to the existing single-family neighborhood. Units would range from studios to three-bedroom homes, with a stated commitment by the applicant to reserve at least 15 percent (approximately 43 units) as deed-restricted affordable housing targeted to low- and moderate-income households. Residential amenities would include shared courtyards, rooftop terraces on the podium buildings, secure bicycle storage rooms, and ground-floor community rooms that could be used for resident meetings, after-school programs, and small fitness or co-working spaces.

The non-residential component would include approximately 63,500 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space and 48,000 square feet of flexible office space. The commercial uses are anticipated to include a small-format grocery store, cafes and restaurants with outdoor seating, a neighborhood pharmacy, and a mix of local-serving retail such as a laundromat, pet supply store, and personal services. The office space would be configured in two 3- to 4-story buildings along E. Riverfront Drive, designed to accommodate professional services, small tech firms, and medical or dental offices. At full buildout, the project is expected to support approximately 400 permanent jobs across commercial and office uses.

A defining feature of the project is the creation of a publicly accessible riverfront promenade and plaza system. The applicant proposes to realign internal circulation so that private vehicles are pulled away from the river’s edge, allowing a continuous multi-use trail (for pedestrians and cyclists) along the top of the existing levee, with landscaped overlooks, seating areas, and interpretive signage about the American River’s ecology and local cultural history. A central public plaza of roughly 0.8 acres would be framed by active ground-floor retail and restaurant uses, with space programmed for seasonal farmers markets, small outdoor concerts, and community events. A 1.5-acre neighborhood park near the northern boundary would provide a playground, half-court basketball area, small dog run, and shaded picnic areas that are intended to serve both new residents and the existing Northside neighborhood.

Vehicular access to the site would be provided primarily from E. Riverfront Drive via a new signalized intersection at 5th Street, along with a secondary right-in/right-out access at 7th Street. The internal circulation plan includes a grid of low-speed shared streets, on-street parking, and clearly marked crosswalks designed to slow traffic and prioritize pedestrian and bicycle movements. The project would construct new sidewalks, street trees, and Class II bike lanes along the project’s frontage on E. Riverfront Drive, and would connect to the existing regional multi-use trail system along the American River levee. Transit stops along E. Riverfront Drive would be upgraded with shelters, seating, and real-time arrival information in coordination with the regional transit agency.

Parking would be provided through a combination of podium garages beneath the apartment buildings, small surface lots behind commercial uses, and on-street spaces throughout the internal street network. The applicant proposes to implement a transportation demand management (TDM) program that may include unbundled residential parking, transit pass subsidies for residents and employees, secure long-term bicycle parking, car-share spaces, and incentives for carpooling.

Infrastructure improvements include new and upsized water, sewer, and storm drainage lines within the project site, with connections to existing city utilities in E. Riverfront Drive and 5th Street. Stormwater would be managed through a network of bioretention planters, permeable paving in selected parking areas, and underground detention facilities to ensure post-project runoff rates do not exceed existing conditions and to improve water quality before discharge to the Riverview Canal and, ultimately, the American River. All buildings would be constructed to meet or exceed current California Building Code and Title 24 energy efficiency standards, with a strong emphasis on sustainability features such as high-efficiency HVAC systems, cool roofs, EV charging stations in residential and commercial parking areas, rooftop solar readiness, and water-efficient landscaping.

Because the site is adjacent to the American River levee and partially within the 100-year floodplain prior to levee certification, the project includes coordination with the local flood control agency to confirm levee integrity and ensure finished floor elevations meet flood protection requirements. Portions of the site currently used as informal outdoor storage would be cleared of debris and potential contaminants, and limited soil remediation may be required where prior industrial activities have left residual petroleum hydrocarbons or metals. The project would implement a construction-phase stormwater pollution prevention plan (SWPPP) and best management practices (BMPs) to control dust, erosion, and runoff during grading and building.

From a planning and entitlement perspective, the project requires adoption of the North Riverfront Mixed-Use Specific Plan, which will establish detailed land use regulations, development standards, and design guidelines for the site; a General Plan Amendment to change the designation from Employment Mixed Use and General Commercial to Riverfront Mixed Use; a Rezone to a new Mixed-Use (MU-RF) zoning district tailored to the specific plan; approval of a Planned Unit Development (PUD) to allow flexible site planning and building heights up to 65 feet in key locations; a Tentative Subdivision Map to create new development parcels, public streets, and open space lots; and one or more Use Permits for outdoor dining, live entertainment in the plaza area, and building height exceptions where necessary. A Development Agreement between the City and the applicant is also proposed to address the timing of infrastructure, affordable housing commitments, and community benefits such as funding for nearby school improvements and contributions to the City’s riverfront trail maintenance program.

The applicant team is North Riverfront Partners, LLC, a local development consortium with prior experience in adaptive reuse and infill projects in Riverview’s downtown. They have held several informal community meetings with nearby residents, business owners, and representatives from Northside Elementary School. Feedback has emphasized the importance of improving lighting and safety along the riverfront, providing family-friendly open space, ensuring that new retail is neighborhood-serving rather than upscale destination-only uses, and minimizing cut-through traffic on existing neighborhood streets. In response, the project design has been revised to include more active ground-floor uses facing the riverfront, a stronger pedestrian connection to Northside Elementary, traffic calming at the northern access point, and a commitment to maintain at least a portion of the commercial space at rents affordable to small local businesses.

Construction is anticipated to occur in two primary phases over approximately 6 to 8 years, with initial work focusing on infrastructure, riverfront promenade construction, and the first mixed-use building along E. Riverfront Drive. Subsequent phases would build out additional residential and commercial components based on market demand, with the intent that key public amenities—the promenade, central plaza, and neighborhood park—be delivered early in the project timeline to serve both new and existing community members. Overall, the project is intended to catalyze reinvestment along the North Riverfront corridor, provide much-needed housing options at a range of price points, and create a vibrant, walkable riverfront district that stitches together the city’s historic downtown, nearby neighborhoods, and the American River open space corridor.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
